caveats: a: BLS occupation data are broader than 221121 and not wage-weighted for the screened firms.
a: The mix between asset owner, independent transmission company, and control-center operator materially changes exposure.
rho: DOE programs demonstrate technical direction, not realized five-year savings in eligible firms.
rho: High-consequence grid operations require validation and cybersecurity controls beyond ordinary enterprise AI.
e: The frozen eight-firm estimate is margin-bridged and highly sensitive to a single classification or size-band error.
e: Establishment NAICS does not reveal whether operating capability and regulated assets reside in a transferable standalone firm.

s5: Affiliate restructurings, passive line interests, and integrated-utility mergers may not qualify under the lens.

q: The source explains rate design but does not measure AI savings or pass-through timing.
d5: Capacity growth is not identical to constant-price operating-service demand.
d5: National needs and plans may benefit large utilities and public entities rather than the small eligible population.
o: Operator consolidation can reduce the number of entities without eliminating the accountable function.
o: Some planning, market coordination, or control responsibilities may migrate to larger regional organizations.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Electric Bulk Power Transmission and Control

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring regulated or contracted transmission and control revenue through FERC-approved tariffs and formula rates, state-regulated arrangements where applicable, network or point-to-point service, interconnection and congestion-related services, or long-term contracts; revenue commonly recovers operating costs, depreciation, taxes, and an allowed return on invested capital.
lens.excluded_activities: Electric generation, final-customer distribution, construction and equipment vendors, engineering consultants without operating responsibility, captive internal departments, government agencies, public or nonprofit system operators outside transferable control ownership, passive asset shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms operating high-voltage electric transmission lines, transformer stations, control centers, or related bulk-power control systems and providing recurring transmission or control service to generators, distribution utilities, load-serving entities, market participants, or other external customers.

a.bridge: BLS describes power distributors and dispatchers across generation, transmission, and distribution rather than a wage-weighted 221121 task inventory. I combine its monitoring, switching, voltage-control, and emergency duties with DOE evidence on AI-enabled grid analytics, then map planning, forecasting, contingency analysis, alarm triage, asset-health review, outage scheduling, interconnection studies, compliance, rate support, and administration into exposed wages; the broad occupation may overstate nontransmission work and understate engineers and planners.
caveats: BLS occupation data are broader than 221121 and not wage-weighted for the screened firms.
The mix between asset owner, independent transmission company, and control-center operator materially changes exposure.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task study at eligible transmission operators covering system operations, electrical engineering, planning, interconnection, asset management, vegetation and field work, maintenance, cybersecurity, compliance, regulatory affairs, finance, and administration, net of current EMS, SCADA, protection, and analytics automation.
a.rationale: Bulk transmission employs extensive data, models, documents, and repetitive analyses that can support AI assistance. Core switching authority, real-time control, field inspection, substation and line maintenance, emergency restoration, cybersecurity judgment, and accountable engineering remain human or physical, and much telemetry and protection is already automated.

d5.bridge: DOE reports planned long-distance transmission expansion and assessed infrastructure need, not a five-year constant-price quantity index for eligible LMM transmission operators. I translate a stated plan to expand long-distance line capacity 16 percent by 2030 and near-term need from load, generation interconnection, and congestion into a positive central case, while discounting for permitting, siting, supply-chain, financing, litigation, and the possibility that additions accrue to large incumbents outside the lens.
caveats: Capacity growth is not identical to constant-price operating-service demand.
National needs and plans may benefit large utilities and public entities rather than the small eligible population.
d5.change_evidence: FERC Form 1 transmission plant and expense by operator, EIA line and substation data, DOE project awards, regional transmission portfolios, ISO and RTO interconnection and load forecasts, approved construction, energization dates, retirements, congestion, and operator-specific rate-base plans.
d5.rationale: Data-center and manufacturing load, generation and load interconnection, congestion relief, severe-weather resilience, and replacement of aging infrastructure support more transmission capacity and control work. Long development timelines, local opposition, cost allocation, transformer constraints, efficiency improvements, and consolidation can delay or redirect growth.

caveats: The frozen eight-firm estimate is margin-bridged and highly sensitive to a single classification or size-band error.
Establishment NAICS does not reveal whether operating capability and regulated assets reside in a transferable standalone firm.
e.change_evidence: Entity-level mapping of SUSB firms to FERC tariff holders, EIA utilities, NERC registrations, RTO and ISO membership, ultimate owners, public or cooperative status, operating employees, transmission miles and substations, EBITDA, and separability of tariffs, control centers, licenses, and customer contracts.
e.rationale: The industry includes recurring external transmission service, but many operators are government, cooperative, nonprofit regional organizations, captive utility divisions, or subsidiaries of large integrated utilities. The frozen LMM count is only eight and may include entities whose assets, tariffs, personnel, or control obligations cannot transfer as an independent operating business.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 221121 ($4,220,298,947) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 221121 ($17,601,105,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.3492 (margins.json[22]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Utility (General) EBITDA/Sales 34.92%); revenue/employee $899,392 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 221121); class <5: 30 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,082,267 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $377,928 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 5 firms, avg revenue/firm $22,534,800 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,869,152 -> IN $1-10M band; class 10-19: 3 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,804,667 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,026,990 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 14 firms, avg revenue/firm $73,951,857 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $25,823,989 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $131,364,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $45,872,309 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 32 firms, avg revenue/firm $475,654,594 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $166,098,584 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 8

caveats: Operator consolidation can reduce the number of entities without eliminating the accountable function.
Some planning, market coordination, or control responsibilities may migrate to larger regional organizations.
o.change_evidence: Regulatory approvals for autonomous control centers, remote fleet consolidation, independent system-operator centralization, customer-owned transmission, outsourced control services, reliability events, NERC registration changes, and insurance or license requirements for unattended operation.
o.rationale: Bulk transmission remains a physical network requiring an accountable operator for real-time voltage and flow control, switching, maintenance, protection, cybersecurity, reliability standards, emergency response, tariffs, and customer access. AI and advanced controls can centralize or reduce staffing but cannot replace the legal and operational entity responsible for the current basket.

q.bridge: FERC describes formula rates that update transmission cost-of-service inputs annually, not the discounted share of AI benefit retained by LMM 221121 firms. I infer relatively rapid pass-through of reduced O&M and other expenses under formula rates, with temporary timing benefits and higher retention for fixed-price, performance-based, merchant, or capacity-expanding arrangements; this may understate retention where savings enable incremental throughput or defer unrecovered costs.
caveats: Formula-rate mechanics differ by operator and jurisdiction.
The source explains rate design but does not measure AI savings or pass-through timing.
q.change_evidence: Operator-specific tariffs and formula-rate protocols, annual updates, true-up lags, O&M sharing, performance incentives, return-on-equity treatment, fixed-price contracts, rate-case calendars, penalty exposure, and regulatory decisions on technology savings.
q.rationale: Cost-of-service and formula-rate regulation is designed to recover prudent costs plus a reasonable return, so recurring operating savings often reduce future revenue requirements rather than accrue indefinitely to owners. Benefit tied to reliability, avoided penalties, faster interconnection, capital deployment, or contract pricing can be retained more fully.

caveats: DOE programs demonstrate technical direction, not realized five-year savings in eligible firms.
High-consequence grid operations require validation and cybersecurity controls beyond ordinary enterprise AI.
rho.change_evidence: Audited utility deployments for contingency screening, load and renewable forecasts, alarm triage, dynamic ratings, vegetation and asset inspection, outage planning, interconnection studies, compliance, and rate-case support, measuring labor hours, false positives, study cycle time, reliability events, restoration time, and avoided maintenance.
rho.rationale: Planning, document review, forecasting, maintenance prioritization, compliance evidence, and operator decision support are implementable within five years. Critical-infrastructure cybersecurity, model validation, integration with legacy EMS and SCADA, explainability, sparse extreme-event data, union and workforce change, regulatory scrutiny, and mandatory human authority constrain deployment.

caveats: No direct qualifying-transfer rate for LMM 221121 firms was found.
Affiliate restructurings, passive line interests, and integrated-utility mergers may not qualify under the lens.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of FERC section 203 applications and notices, state commission approvals, EIA ownership changes, PitchBook and infrastructure-fund transactions matched to a verified eligible denominator and screened for completed control transfer of a standalone operating platform.
s5.rationale: Transmission assets and utilities do change ownership, but qualifying control sales of small independent operating firms are constrained by strategic infrastructure, public and cooperative ownership, incumbent utility scale, tariff and license transfer, customer and regulator review, and a tiny eligible denominator. FERC review above statutory thresholds makes larger transactions observable but does not establish a five-year rate.

principal_driver: Rising load, grid congestion, interconnection, and resilience needs support durable transmission and control demand alongside rich AI-addressable planning and asset data.
principal_weakness: The tiny mostly regulated target population combines low firm availability with formula-rate pass-through that limits retained operating savings.
